critique.summary: The FSFE announced that their long-term payment provider Nexi terminated their contract without prior notice, halting recurring donations from over 450 supporters. FSFE refused Nexi's request for sensitive supporter data, which they deemed unnecessary and illegal, leading to the contract cancellation and disruption of donations.
mainPoints: Nexi terminated FSFE's payment processing contract abruptly, affecting over 450 recurring donors.
Nexi requested sensitive supporter data, including usernames and passwords, which FSFE refused to provide.
FSFE was not informed of any deadline to comply with Nexi's data request before contract termination.
FSFE has transitioned to a new payment provider but cannot automatically migrate existing supporter accounts.
FSFE expressed concern about losing financial support due to this disruption and criticized Nexi's demands as incomprehensible and invasive.
critique.truthfulness: The claims made by FSFE are consistent with typical contractual disputes between organizations and payment providers regarding data privacy and compliance. Nexi's request for usernames and passwords is unusual and generally considered a violation of privacy and security best practices, supporting FSFE's refusal. The timeline and impact on recurring donations are plausible and align with standard payment processing operations. However, independent confirmation from Nexi or third-party sources is not provided in the article, limiting verification of Nexi's perspective or contractual terms.
considerations: The article does not include Nexi's response or perspective on the contract termination and data requests.
It is unclear whether Nexi's request for supporter data was legally justified under applicable regulations or contractual terms.
The potential impact on FSFE's financial stability and ongoing operations due to the donation disruption is not quantified.
The article does not specify the new payment provider or measures taken to secure supporter data going forward.

comments_critique.summary: The discussion centers on FSFE's refusal to provide private supporter data, specifically usernames and passwords, to their payment provider Nexi, which led to the termination of their service. Commenters debate the plausibility of Nexi's request, the implications for privacy and security, and broader reflections on bureaucracy and alternative payment methods like Bitcoin.

comments.author: eequah9L
comments.text:
> Over the past few months, our former payment provider Nexi S.p.A. (“Nexi”) requested access to private data, which we understood to be specifically the usernames and passwords of our supporters.

I must be missing something, but why is there an expectation that clear text passwords would even be known?
comments.analysis: This comment correctly questions the technical feasibility of a company having access to plaintext passwords, highlighting a common security best practice that passwords should be stored hashed.

comments.author: Freak_NL
comments.text: The FSFE justly drew the line at providing private information of supporters. How many other customers of Nexi simply handed over such data 'because audit'?
comments.analysis: This comment supports FSFE's stance on privacy and raises an important question about how other customers might have complied without scrutiny, emphasizing the importance of data protection.

comments.author: janpio
comments.text: So what did Nexi really want, and how did it get mangled so badly that it came out as "specifically the usernames and passwords of our supporters"?
comments.analysis: This comment thoughtfully questions the communication and interpretation of Nexi's request, suggesting possible misunderstandings or misrepresentations in the reported demands.

comments.author: samsk
comments.text:
We work with MLS provider(s) that requires us to keep plaintext password for our users and provide it on request in case of `breach in the security of MLS Listing Information or a violation of MLS Rules`.

The user is accessing only copy of their data in _our_ systems, the user has no contact with MLS itself directly or indirectly.
comments.analysis: This comment provides a real-world example where plaintext passwords are retained due to external requirements, adding nuance to the discussion about password handling practices.

comments.author: butokai
comments.text: As an Italian living in another EU country, I always thought that the amount of (broken) bureaucracy of Italy was not particularly worse. However this story comes after a couple more I heard this week, in a line of absurd practice possibly due to absurd regulations.
comments.analysis: This comment adds context about bureaucratic challenges in Italy, which may contribute to the situation, though it is more anecdotal and less directly related to the technical aspects.

comments.author: sam_lowry_
comments.text:
Reminds me of the famous "Our security auditor is an idiot. How do I give him the information he wants? [1]

[1] https://serverfault.com/questions/293217/our-security-audito...
comments.analysis: This comment uses humor and sarcasm to criticize the situation but does not add substantive analysis or factual information.

comments.author: littlecranky67
comments.text: Everytime people say bitcoin has no use case, I'd like to point them to cases like this.
comments.analysis: This comment promotes Bitcoin as a solution without addressing the specific issues raised, making it less relevant and more of a promotional remark.

comments.author: grigio
comments.text: Maybe now more F/OSS supporters will understand the need of Bitcoin/Monero
comments.analysis: Similar to the previous comment, this one advocates for cryptocurrency adoption without engaging with the core discussion, reducing its constructive value.
